name: seL4 — Verified Capability Microkernel
summary: Klein et al. (NICTA / Data61 / Trustworthy Systems): the first general-purpose OS kernel with formal proof of authority confinement (Isabelle/HOL refinement down to C). Every kernel object (TCB, CNode, frame, endpoint) is named only by a capability stored in a CSpace; user-level *retypes* untyped memory into kernel objects. Production deployment in safety-critical and defence systems.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**seL4** (Klein et al., NICTA / Data61, now Trustworthy Systems / UNSW Sydney) is a third-generation L4 microkernel with a formally verified capability-based authority discipline. It is the first general-purpose OS kernel with a machine-checked proof of authority confinement: the Isabelle/HOL proof refines an abstract specification down to the C implementation (and an executable binary).

Every kernel object — TCBs (thread control blocks), CNodes (capability containers), memory frames, endpoints, IRQ handlers — is named only by a **capability stored in a CSpace** (capability space). There is no kernel-side global object table accessible by name; an operation invokes a capability the caller holds, and the kernel checks the rights bits before performing the requested action.

sections.title: Untyped capabilities and retype
sections.content:
**Untyped capabilities + retype**: the kernel never allocates implicitly. All in-kernel memory consumption is *authorised* by an Untyped capability, which a user-level component can `Retype` into kernel objects (a TCB, a CNode, a frame, etc.). After retype, the Untyped capability shrinks to cover the unused remainder; the new typed capabilities are placed into the caller's CSpace.

This pushes memory-allocation policy entirely outside the verified kernel: the kernel has no malloc, no global free list, no allocator. The proof of confinement is correspondingly simpler — there is no kernel-managed memory pool that could leak between principals.

**Reply capabilities**: a single-use return token, generated at IPC call time and consumed on reply. This makes RPC exactly-once and revocation-safe — a server cannot accidentally reply twice, and a caller cannot use a stale reply capability.

sections.title: Verification and production deployment
sections.content:
**Formal proof**: Isabelle/HOL refinement chain from an abstract specification through an executable specification to the actual C implementation, with binary verification connecting C to optimised ARM/RISC-V binary. Take-grant-style authority bound proven on the actual implementation — a take-grant graph derivable from the static configuration is the upper bound on authority that any execution can attain.

Status (as of 2026-04): seL4 deployed in safety-critical and defence systems (Boeing autonomous drones, DARPA HACMS demonstrators, the Trustworthy Systems' SeL4 Foundation members include Cisco, HENSOLDT Cyber, NIO automotive, etc.). Production-grade for the niche but not a general-purpose desktop kernel.

seL4 is the canonical citation for "verified capability microkernel" and the existence proof that authority-confinement proofs scale to a real OS kernel. Coyotos was an attempted successor (Shapiro et al., 2006) that did not reach production; seL4 won.
